title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 6 (Fragment 4)
text:
On nearly every page, the printing had melted away, leaving a slimy, leathery surface quite unlike paper, with only a mark or so left in the middle. Old Niccolo looked closely at each mark and grunted. He grunted again at the first picture, which had faded like the print, but left a clearer mark. After that, though there was no print on any of the pages, the remaining mark was steadily clearer, up to the center of the book, when it began to become more faded again, until the mark was barely visible on the back page.

Old Niccolo laid down the pen and the tongs in terrible silence. “Right through,” he said at length. People shifted and someone coughed, but nobody said anything. “I do not know,” said Old Niccolo, “the substance this object is made of, but I know a calling-charm when I see one. Tonino must have been like one hypnotized, if he had read all this.”

“He was a bit strange at breakfast,” Paolo whispered.

“I am sure he was,” said his grandfather. He looked reflectively at the shriveled stump of the book and then around at the crowded faces of his family. “Now who,” he asked softly, “would want to set a strong calling-charm on Tonino Montana? Who would be mean enough to pick on a child? Who would—?” He turned suddenly on Benvenuto, crouched beside the book, and Benvenuto cowered right down, quivering, with his ragged ears flat against his flat head. “Where were you last night, Benvenuto?” he asked, more softly still.

No one understood the reply Benvenuto gave as he cowered, but everyone knew the answer. It was in Antonio and Elizabeth’s harrowed faces, in the set of Rinaldo’s chin, in Aunt Francesca’s narrowed eyes, narrowed almost out of existence, and in the way Aunt Maria looked at Uncle Lorenzo; but most of all, it was in the way Benvenuto threw himself down on his side, with his back to the room, the picture of a cat in despair.

Old Niccolo looked up. “Now isn’t that odd?” he said gently. “Benvenuto spent last night chasing a white she-cat—over the roofs of the Casa Petrocchi.” He paused to let that sink in. “So Benvenuto,” he said, “who knows a bad spell when he sees one, was not around to warn Tonino.”

“But why ?” Elizabeth asked despairingly.

Old Niccolo went, if possible, quieter still. “I can only conclude, my dear, that the Petrocchis are being paid by Florence, Siena, or Pisa.”

There was another silence, thick and meaningful. Antonio broke it. “Well,” he said, in such a subdued, grim way that Paolo stared at him. “Well? Are we going?”

“Of course,” said Old Niccolo. “Domenico, fetch me my small black spell-book.”

Everyone left the room, so suddenly, quietly and purposefully that Paolo was left behind, not clear what was going on. He turned uncertainly to go to the door, and realized that Rosa had been left behind too. She was sitting on Tonino’s bed, with one hand to her head, white as Tonino’s sheets.

“Paolo,” she said, “tell Claudia I’ll have the baby, if she wants to go. I’ll have all the little ones.”

She looked up at Paolo as she said it, and she looked so strange that Paolo was suddenly frightened. He ran gladly out into the gallery. The family was gathering, still quiet and grim, in the yard. Paolo ran down there and gave his message. Protesting little ones were pushed up the steps to Rosa, but Paolo did not help. He found Elizabeth and Lucia and pushed close to them. Elizabeth put an arm around him and an arm around Lucia.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell meant to attract or compel someone.
vocabulary.example: I do not know the substance this object is made of, but I know a calling-charm when I see one.

vocabulary.definition: To shrink back or crouch down in fear.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto cowered right down, quivering, with his ragged ears flat against his flat head.

vocabulary.definition: Bent down low with the limbs close to the body.
vocabulary.example: He turned suddenly on Benvenuto, crouched beside the book.

vocabulary.definition: Complete loss or absence of hope.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto threw himself down, the picture of a cat in despair.

vocabulary.definition: Gradually lost brightness or clarity.
vocabulary.example: He grunted again at the first picture, which had faded like the print.

vocabulary.definition: Afraid or anxious.
vocabulary.example: He looked so strange that Paolo was suddenly frightened.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people assembled together.
vocabulary.example: The family was gathering, still quiet and grim, in the yard.

vocabulary.definition: Forbidding or uninviting; gloomy or depressing.
vocabulary.example: He spoke in such a subdued, grim way.

vocabulary.definition: Made a low guttural sound expressing dissatisfaction or effort.
vocabulary.example: Old Niccolo looked closely at each mark and grunted.

vocabulary.definition: Distressed or disturbed, especially emotionally.
vocabulary.example: It was in Antonio and Elizabeth’s harrowed faces.

vocabulary.definition: Affected as if under hypnosis; in a trance-like state.
vocabulary.example: Tonino must have been like one hypnotized, if he had read all this.

vocabulary.definition: Having a tough, flexible texture similar to leather.
vocabulary.example: On nearly every page, the printing had melted away, leaving a slimy, leathery surface quite unlike paper.

vocabulary.definition: Became smaller in width or became more focused/intense (as with eyes).
vocabulary.example: in the narrowed eyes of Aunt Francesca.

vocabulary.definition: Expressing objection or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: Protesting little ones were pushed up the steps to Rosa.

vocabulary.definition: With intention and determination.
vocabulary.example: Everyone left the room suddenly, quietly and purposefully.

vocabulary.definition: More silent or calm.
vocabulary.example: Old Niccolo went, if possible, quieter still.

vocabulary.definition: Shaking or trembling slightly, typically due to fear or cold.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto cowered right down, quivering, with his ragged ears flat against his flat head.

vocabulary.definition: Having a rough, irregular, or uneven edge or surface.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto cowered with his ragged ears flat against his flat head.

vocabulary.definition: In a thoughtful or contemplative manner.
vocabulary.example: He looked reflectively at the shriveled stump of the book and then around at the crowded faces of his family.

vocabulary.definition: Wrinkled and contracted, especially due to loss of moisture or age.
vocabulary.example: He looked reflectively at the shriveled stump of the book.

vocabulary.definition: Complete absence of sound or speech.
vocabulary.example: Old Niccolo laid down the pen and the tongs in terrible silence.

vocabulary.definition: A book containing magical spells.
vocabulary.example: “Domenico, fetch me my small black spell-book.”

vocabulary.definition: In a regular, even, or continuous manner.
vocabulary.example: After that, though there was no print on any of the pages, the remaining mark was steadily clearer.

vocabulary.definition: Quiet and rather reflective or depressed.
vocabulary.example: Antonio spoke in such a subdued, grim way that Paolo stared at him.

vocabulary.definition: The material of which something is made.
vocabulary.example: I do not know the substance this object is made of.
